description: Procedural law for administration of criminal law in India

sections.title: When police may arrest without warrant
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: Any police officer may without an order from a Magistrate and without a warrant, arrest any person who has been concerned in any cognizable offence, or against whom a reasonable complaint has been made, or credible information has been received, or a reasonable suspicion exists, of his having been so concerned.
sections.simplified: Police can arrest someone without a warrant if: they have committed a cognizable offence, there's a credible complaint against them, or there's reasonable suspicion. However, for offences with up to 7 years punishment, arrest should be a last resort.

sections.title: Notice of appearance before police officer
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: The police officer shall, in all cases where the arrest of a person is not required under the provisions of sub-section (1) of section 41, issue a notice directing the person against whom a reasonable complaint has been made to appear before him or at such other place as may be specified in the notice.
sections.simplified: Instead of arresting you, the police can issue a notice asking you to appear before them. You must comply with this notice. If you don't, they can arrest you.

sections.title: Arrest how made
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: In making an arrest the police officer or other person making the same shall actually touch or confine the body of the person to be arrested, unless there be a submission to the custody by word or action. No woman shall be arrested after sunset and before sunrise except in exceptional circumstances, and where such exceptional circumstances exist, the woman police officer shall, by making a written report, obtain the prior permission of the Judicial Magistrate.
sections.simplified: During arrest, the officer must touch or physically confine the person. Women CANNOT be arrested between sunset and sunrise except in exceptional cases, and only with a woman police officer and Magistrate's written permission.

sections.title: No unnecessary restraint
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: The person arrested shall not be subjected to more restraint than is necessary to prevent his escape.
sections.simplified: The police cannot use excessive force during or after arrest. They can only use the minimum force needed to prevent you from escaping.

sections.title: Person arrested to be informed of grounds of arrest and of right to bail
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: Every police officer or other person arresting any person without warrant shall forthwith communicate to him full particulars of the offence for which he is arrested or other grounds for such arrest. Where a police officer arrests without warrant any person other than a person accused of a non-bailable offence, he shall inform the person arrested that he is entitled to be released on bail.
sections.simplified: When arrested, the police MUST tell you: 1) Why you are being arrested, and 2) If your offence is bailable, they must inform you of your right to bail.

sections.title: Obligation of person making arrest to inform about the arrest
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: Every police officer or other person making any arrest under this Code shall forthwith give the information regarding such arrest and place where the arrested person is being held to any of his friends, relatives or such other persons as may be disclosed or nominated by the arrested person.
sections.simplified: When someone is arrested, the police MUST immediately inform the person's family member, friend, or anyone nominated by the arrested person about: 1) The arrest, and 2) Where the person is being held.

sections.title: Health and safety of arrested person
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: It shall be the duty of the person having the custody of an accused to take reasonable care of the health and safety of the accused.
sections.simplified: The police must take proper care of your health and safety when you are in their custody. They must provide medical attention if needed.

sections.title: Person arrested not to be detained more than twenty-four hours
sections.chapter: V - Arrest of Persons
sections.text: No police officer shall detain in custody a person arrested without warrant for a longer period than under all the circumstances of the case is reasonable, and such period shall not, in the absence of a special order of a Magistrate under section 167, exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.
sections.simplified: Police CANNOT keep you in custody for more than 24 hours without producing you before a Magistrate. This 24-hour period excludes travel time to the court.

sections.title: Order for maintenance of wives, children and parents
sections.chapter: IX - Order for Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents
sections.text: If any person having sufficient means neglects or refuses to maintain his wife, unable to maintain herself, or his legitimate or illegitimate minor child, or his legitimate or illegitimate child who has attained the age of majority and is unable to maintain himself due to physical or mental abnormality, or his father or mother unable to maintain himself or herself, a Magistrate of the first class may order such person to make a monthly allowance for the maintenance of his wife, child, father or mother.
sections.simplified: A person must financially support: 1) their wife if she can't support herself, 2) their minor or disabled children, 3) their elderly parents if they can't support themselves. A Magistrate can order monthly maintenance payments.

sections.title: Power to issue order in urgent cases of nuisance or apprehended danger
sections.chapter: X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquility
sections.text: If a District Magistrate, a Sub-divisional Magistrate, or any other Executive Magistrate specially empowered considers that there is sufficient ground for proceeding and immediate prevention or speedy remedy is desirable, he may issue an order directing any person to abstain from a certain act or to take certain order with respect to certain property in his possession or under his management.
sections.simplified: Section 144 allows a Magistrate to issue emergency orders to prevent public disturbance. This is commonly used to ban gatherings of more than 4 people, impose curfews, or restrict certain activities in an area.

sections.title: Information in cognizable cases (FIR)
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence, if given orally to an officer in charge of a police station, shall be reduced to writing by him or under his direction, and be read over to the informant, and every such information, whether given in writing or reduced to writing as aforesaid, shall be signed by the person giving it, and the substance thereof shall be entered in a book to be kept by such officer in such form as the State Government may prescribe in this behalf.
sections.simplified: When you report a cognizable offence (serious crime like theft, assault, murder) at a police station, the officer MUST: 1) Write down your complaint (FIR), 2) Read it back to you, 3) Get your signature, 4) Give you a free copy. The police CANNOT refuse to file an FIR for a cognizable offence.

sections.title: Information as to non-cognizable cases and investigation of such cases
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: When information is given to an officer in charge of a police station of the commission of a non-cognizable offence, he shall enter or cause to be entered the substance of the information in a book to be kept for that purpose and refer the informant to the Magistrate.
sections.simplified: For non-cognizable offences (less serious crimes), the police will record your complaint in a register (NCR - Non-Cognizable Report) and direct you to a Magistrate. Police cannot investigate non-cognizable cases without Magistrate's permission.

sections.title: Police officer's power to investigate cognizable cases
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Any officer in charge of a police station may, without the order of a Magistrate, investigate any cognizable case which a Court having jurisdiction over the local area within the limits of such station would have power to inquire into or try. Any Magistrate empowered under section 190 may order such an investigation.
sections.simplified: Police can investigate cognizable cases (serious crimes) without Magistrate's order. If police refuse to investigate, you can approach a Magistrate under Section 156(3) to order the police to investigate and file an FIR.

sections.title: Police officer's power to require attendance of witnesses
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Any police officer making an investigation may require the attendance before himself of any person within the limits of his own or any adjoining station. No male person under the age of fifteen years or above the age of sixty-five years or a woman or a mentally or physically disabled person shall be required to attend at any place other than the place in which such male person or woman resides.
sections.simplified: Police can summon witnesses during investigation. However, boys under 15, men over 65, women, and disabled persons must be questioned at their residence only — police cannot force them to come to the station.

sections.title: Examination of witnesses by police
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Any police officer making an investigation may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case. Such person shall be bound to answer truly all questions relating to such case put to him by such officer, other than questions the answers to which would have a tendency to expose him to a criminal charge or to a penalty or forfeiture.
sections.simplified: During investigation, police can question witnesses who must answer truthfully. BUT you have the right to refuse to answer questions that might incriminate yourself (self-incrimination protection).

sections.title: Recording of confessions and statements
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Any Metropolitan Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate may, whether or not he has jurisdiction in the case, record any confession or statement made to him in the course of an investigation or at any time afterwards but before the commencement of the inquiry or trial.
sections.simplified: Confessions and important statements must be recorded before a Magistrate (not police) to be valid in court. Confessions made to the police alone are NOT admissible as evidence.

sections.title: Procedure when investigation cannot be completed in twenty-four hours
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Whenever any person is arrested and detained in custody, and it appears that the investigation cannot be completed within the period of twenty-four hours, the officer in charge of the police station shall transmit to the nearest Judicial Magistrate a copy of the entries in the diary relating to the case, and shall at the same time forward the accused to such Magistrate. The Magistrate may authorise the detention of the accused in police custody up to 15 days, and judicial custody up to 60 days for lesser offences and 90 days for offences punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for not less than 10 years.
sections.simplified: If police can't complete investigation in 24 hours, they must take you before a Magistrate. The Magistrate can authorize: Police custody up to 15 days, Judicial custody up to 60 days (minor) or 90 days (serious). After this, you get DEFAULT BAIL.

sections.title: Report of police officer on completion of investigation (Chargesheet)
sections.chapter: XII - Information to the Police and their Powers to Investigate
sections.text: Every investigation shall be completed without unnecessary delay. As soon as it is completed, the officer in charge of the police station shall forward to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence on a police report, a report in the form prescribed by the State Government.
sections.simplified: After investigation, police must file a chargesheet (final report) to the Magistrate. This report includes evidence, witness details, and the police's findings. If police find no evidence, they file a 'closure report' instead.

sections.title: Cognizance of offences by Magistrates
sections.chapter: XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
sections.text: Any Magistrate of the first class, and any Magistrate of the second class specially empowered in this behalf, may take cognizance of any offence upon receiving a complaint, upon a police report, or upon information received from any person other than a police officer.
sections.simplified: A Magistrate can start legal proceedings based on: 1) A direct complaint from a citizen, 2) A police chargesheet, or 3) Information from any person. This means even if police won't help, you can directly approach a Magistrate.

sections.title: Examination of complainant
sections.chapter: XV - Of Complaints to Magistrates
sections.text: A Magistrate taking cognizance of an offence on complaint shall examine upon oath the complainant and the witnesses present, if any, and the substance of such examination shall be reduced to writing and shall be signed by the complainant and the witnesses.
sections.simplified: When you file a complaint directly with a Magistrate, you will be questioned under oath. Your statement and those of your witnesses will be recorded in writing and signed.

sections.title: Judgment of acquittal or conviction
sections.chapter: XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
sections.text: After hearing arguments and points of law, if any, the Judge shall give a judgment of acquittal or conviction. If the accused is convicted, the Judge shall hear the accused on the question of sentence, and then pass sentence on him according to law.
sections.simplified: After the trial, the judge delivers a verdict: acquittal (not guilty) or conviction (guilty). If convicted, you get to speak before sentencing. The judge then decides the punishment according to law.

sections.title: Power to examine the accused
sections.chapter: XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
sections.text: In every inquiry or trial, for the purpose of enabling the accused personally to explain any circumstances appearing in the evidence against him, the Court may at any stage, without previously warning the accused, put such questions to him as the Court considers necessary. The accused shall not render himself liable to punishment by refusing to answer such questions, or by giving false answers to them.
sections.simplified: The court can question the accused to get their explanation of the evidence. Importantly, the accused CANNOT be punished for refusing to answer or giving wrong answers during this examination. This protects the right against self-incrimination.

sections.title: Appeals from convictions
sections.chapter: XXIX - Appeals
sections.text: Any person convicted on a trial held by a High Court may appeal to the Supreme Court. Any person convicted on a trial held by a Sessions Judge or an Additional Sessions Judge may appeal to the High Court. Any person convicted on a trial held by any other Court may appeal to the Court of Session.
sections.simplified: If you're convicted, you have the right to appeal: From Sessions Court → High Court. From High Court → Supreme Court. You can challenge both the conviction and the severity of the sentence.

sections.title: In what cases bail to be taken (Bailable Offences)
sections.chapter: XXXIII - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
sections.text: When any person other than a person accused of a non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station, or appears or is brought before a Court, and is prepared at any time while in the custody of such officer or at any stage of the proceeding before such Court to give bail, such person shall be released on bail.
sections.simplified: For BAILABLE offences, bail is a RIGHT, not a privilege. The police station itself must grant bail — you don't need to go to court. If the police refuse, you can approach the Magistrate who MUST grant bail.

sections.title: When bail may be taken in case of non-bailable offence
sections.chapter: XXXIII - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
sections.text: When any person accused of, or suspected of, the commission of any non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station or appears or is brought before a Court other than the High Court or Court of Session, he may be released on bail, but shall not be so released if there appear reasonable grounds for believing that he has been guilty of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life.
sections.simplified: For NON-BAILABLE offences, bail is at the judge's discretion (not a right). The court considers: nature of crime, evidence strength, flight risk, and whether you might tamper with evidence. For offences punishable with death/life imprisonment, bail is harder to get.

sections.title: Direction for grant of bail to person apprehending arrest (Anticipatory Bail)
sections.chapter: XXXIII - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
sections.text: Where any person has reason to believe that he may be arrested on accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence, he may apply to the High Court or the Court of Session for a direction under this section that in the event of such arrest, he shall be released on bail.
sections.simplified: ANTICIPATORY BAIL: If you fear you might be arrested, you can apply to the Sessions Court or High Court for advance bail. If granted, you will be released immediately upon arrest without spending time in jail.

sections.title: Special powers of High Court or Court of Session regarding bail
sections.chapter: XXXIII - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
sections.text: A High Court or Court of Session may direct that any person accused of an offence and in custody be released on bail, and if the offence is of the nature specified in sub-section (3) of section 437, may impose any condition which it considers necessary.
sections.simplified: The High Court and Sessions Court have special powers to grant bail even in serious cases. You can approach these courts if the lower court refuses bail.

sections.title: Maximum period for which an undertrial prisoner can be detained
sections.chapter: XXXIII - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
sections.text: Where a person has, during the period of investigation, inquiry or trial under this Code of an offence under any law been detained for a period extending up to half of the maximum period of imprisonment specified for that offence under that law, he shall be released by the Court on his personal bond with or without sureties.
sections.simplified: If you have been in jail as an undertrial for HALF the maximum sentence period for your offence, you MUST be released on bail. This prevents people from spending more time in jail waiting for trial than the actual sentence.

sections.title: Order for custody and disposal of property pending trial
sections.chapter: XXXIV - Disposal of Property
sections.text: When any property is produced before any Criminal Court during any inquiry or trial, the Court may make such order as it thinks fit for the proper custody of such property pending the conclusion of the inquiry or trial.
sections.simplified: During a trial, the court decides how to handle property involved in the case (like stolen goods, vehicles, etc.). The court can order the property to be kept safely until the case is resolved.

sections.title: Procedure by police upon seizure of property
sections.chapter: XXXIV - Disposal of Property
sections.text: Whenever the seizure of property by any police officer is reported to a Magistrate, and such property is not produced before a Criminal Court during an inquiry or trial, the Magistrate may direct the property to be restored to the person from whom it was seized.
sections.simplified: If police seize your property and it's not needed for a trial, you can apply to a Magistrate to get it back. The Magistrate can order the police to return your property.

sections.title: Bar to taking cognizance after lapse of the period of limitation
sections.chapter: XXXVI - Limitation for Taking Cognizance
sections.text: No Court shall take cognizance of an offence after the expiry of the period of limitation: 6 months for offences punishable with fine only, 1 year for offences punishable up to 1 year, 3 years for offences punishable between 1 to 3 years. For more serious offences, there is no time limitation.
sections.simplified: There are time limits for filing complaints: Fine-only offences: 6 months. Up to 1 year jail offences: 1 year. 1-3 year jail offences: 3 years. Serious offences (3+ years jail): No time limit. Act quickly!
